title: Three-engine selection via string dispatch in common.py, not a plugin registry
description: Engine selection is done by string matching on a `backend` parameter passed at call time. `common.py` uses `importlib.import_module` for hybrid (deferred to avoid torch dependency on pure-client installs) and direct imports for vlm/pipeline. There is no factory class or plugin registry — the caller passes `backend='pipeline'|'vlm-auto-engine'|'hybrid-http-client'` etc. and `do_parse`/`aio_do_parse` branch on that string. Each engine lives in its own `mineru/backend/{pipeline,vlm,hybrid}/` subpackage with identical `doc_analyze(pdf_bytes, image_writer, ...)` and `aio_doc_analyze(...)` signatures, making the interface contract implicit (duck-typed) rather than enforced by an ABC.
relevance_notes: Direct lift: string-keyed dispatch with deferred importlib.import_module is a clean pattern for optional-dependency backends. The identical sync/async signature pair across all backends is worth copying.

title: Keyed Singleton with (backend, model_path, server_url) tuple as cache key
description: Both VLM and Pipeline backends implement a `ModelSingleton` using a class-level dict keyed by a tuple of init params. The singleton is created with `__new__` + `threading.RLock` (VLM) or `PIPELINE_MODEL_INIT_LOCK` (pipeline). This allows multiple distinct model configs to coexist in the same process. `atexit.register(shutdown_cached_models)` ensures GPU memory is released on process exit. The VLM singleton also attaches `_mineru_runtime_handles` dict to each predictor for deterministic multi-path shutdown traversal.
relevance_notes: The tuple-keyed cache pattern is directly portable to any service that needs process-lifetime singletons with multiple config variants. The `_mineru_runtime_handles` + multi-path shutdown probe pattern (`shutdown`, `close`, `stop`, `terminate`, `engine.shutdown`, ...) is worth extracting for any framework that wraps third-party inference engines with unknown teardown APIs.

title: Sliding window (processing_window_size) applied uniformly across all three backends
description: `get_processing_window_size(default=64)` is read from config and used identically in pipeline_analyze.py (line 180), vlm_analyze.py (line 424), and hybrid_analyze.py (line 567). Each backend iterates `range(0, page_count, effective_window_size)`, loads only that page slice into PIL Images, runs inference, appends to middle_json, then frees the image batch (`_close_images`). Peak memory is bounded to `window_size` pages worth of PIL images at any moment regardless of total document length. The pipeline backend extends this further with a multi-doc variant (`doc_analyze_streaming`) that interleaves pages from multiple documents into a single window.
relevance_notes: Extremely portable. Any multi-page inference pipeline should adopt this configurable-window-size pattern. The explicit `_close_images` call after each window prevents PIL image accumulation — critical for long-running services.

title: Async task API backed by in-memory dict + asyncio.Queue, no Redis
description: `AsyncTaskManager` stores tasks in a plain `dict[str, AsyncParseTask]` and uses an `asyncio.Queue[str]` for the work queue. States are string constants: `pending`, `processing`, `completed`, `failed`. A `_dispatcher_loop` coroutine dequeues task IDs and acquires a `asyncio.Semaphore` to enforce `max_concurrent_requests`. Callers poll `GET /tasks/{id}` or await an `asyncio.Event` per task. A background `_cleanup_loop` TTL-expires completed tasks (default 24h). Output is written to the filesystem under `{output_root}/{task_id}/`. This is entirely in-process — no Redis, no Celery, no persistence across restarts.
relevance_notes: The in-memory + filesystem pattern is clean for single-node deployments but cannot survive process restarts. Extractable for services where tasks are short-lived and restart resilience is not required. The `asyncio.Event` per task for synchronous wait (used by the `file_parse` sync endpoint) is a clean pattern.

title: Router uses load-score routing: (queued+processing+pending)/max_concurrent, jitter via shuffle
description: `WorkerState.score()` computes `(queued_tasks + processing_tasks + pending_assignments) / max_concurrent_requests`. Selection in `acquire_submission_server` first shuffles candidates (randomizer.shuffle) then stable-sorts by `(score, pending_assignments, source_preference)` where local workers are preferred over remote. `pending_assignments` is incremented optimistically before the HTTP call lands, preventing thundering-herd assignment to a single worker. A `asyncio.Lock` on `_selection_lock` serializes selections. Health is polled every 2 seconds via background `_monitor_loop`. Consecutive failures >= 2 trigger auto-restart for local workers (subprocess management).
relevance_notes: The score-then-jitter pattern (shuffle + stable sort) prevents all ties going to the same server while still respecting load. The optimistic `pending_assignments` increment before HTTP confirmation is a key subtlety worth copying. Auto-restart of child processes via consecutive-failure threshold is directly portable.

title: Router spawns local workers as subprocesses with per-GPU CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES isolation
description: The `ManagedLocalServer` dataclass spawns `mineru.cli.fast_api` as a child subprocess via `subprocess.Popen`, setting `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES={gpu}` in the child environment. Each GPU gets its own isolated API process. The router auto-detects available GPUs via `torch.cuda.device_count()` or respects `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` from the parent environment. This means GPU isolation is achieved by OS-level env var scoping, not CUDA MPS or any framework-level partitioning.
relevance_notes: Simple and effective. The subprocess-per-GPU pattern avoids CUDA context conflicts between workers. The `find_free_port()` + subprocess approach is directly reusable for any multi-GPU inference service without requiring Kubernetes or Ray.

title: MLX backend gets a threading.Lock execution guard; async variant uses asyncio.to_thread(lock.acquire)
description: Apple MLX does not support concurrent execution. `_maybe_enable_serial_execution` attaches a `threading.Lock` to the predictor object if and only if the backend is `mlx-engine`. A context manager `predictor_execution_guard` wraps inference calls — no-op for non-MLX, locking for MLX. The async variant `aio_predictor_execution_guard` uses `asyncio.to_thread(lock.acquire)` to avoid blocking the event loop. This is a per-object, opt-in serialization pattern rather than a global lock.
relevance_notes: The pattern of attaching a lock as an attribute to a shared object and using a no-op context manager when not needed is cleaner than wrapping all call sites in conditionals. Directly extractable for any backend that has concurrency constraints.

title: Hybrid engine combines VLM layout detection with pipeline OCR as sidecar fusion
description: The hybrid backend runs VLM inference to get block layout (tables, images, equations), then conditionally invokes pipeline OCR models (`HybridModelSingleton`) for text extraction and inline formula recognition. `mask_image_regions` whites out VLM-handled blocks before OCR to prevent double-processing. Results from VLM and OCR are merged via `_merge_page_sidecar_items`. The `_should_enable_vlm_ocr` function gates whether VLM or pipeline OCR handles scanned text, based on language, formula_enable flag, and env vars (`MINERU_FORCE_VLM_OCR_ENABLE`, `MINERU_HYBRID_FORCE_PIPELINE_ENABLE`).
relevance_notes: The pattern of using one model for detection/layout and a second model for content extraction, with region masking to prevent overlap, is broadly applicable to document AI pipelines. The env-var feature flags for forcing specific sub-paths are a good production operations pattern.

title: DataReader/DataWriter ABC with four concrete implementations: filebase, S3, multi-bucket-S3, dummy
description: `DataReader` defines `read(path)` and `read_at(path, offset, limit)`. `DataWriter` defines `write(path, data)` and `write_string(path, data)` with a UTF-8/ASCII fallback encoder. Four concrete implementations exist: `FileBasedDataReader/Writer`, `S3DataReader/Writer`, `MultiBucketS3DataReader/Writer`, `DummyDataWriter`. All image writes across all backends go through the injected `image_writer: DataWriter | None` parameter. This means S3-backed deployments require zero changes to inference code — only the writer instance changes.
relevance_notes: Clean IO abstraction with the dummy writer for tests is directly portable. The `write_string` UTF-8 fallback with silent ASCII degradation is pragmatic for international content. The `read_at(offset, limit)` signature anticipates range reads for large files — useful for S3.

title: Single Python package, no monorepo — pyproject.toml optional-deps encode the engine split
description: MinerU ships as a single `mineru` package (`include=['mineru*']`). The three engines are encoded as optional dependency groups: `pipeline` (torch + onnxruntime + shapely), `vlm` (torch + transformers), `vllm` (vllm), `lmdeploy` (lmdeploy), `mlx` (mlx-vlm, macOS only). `all` installs the full stack with platform guards (`sys_platform == 'darwin'` for mlx, `linux` for vllm, `win32` for lmdeploy). There are no separate packages or wheels per engine. The `projects/` directory does not exist in this repo.
relevance_notes: Conceptual: encoding hardware-specific inference backends as optional dep groups with platform markers is a clean single-package distribution strategy. No separate repos needed for CPU vs GPU variants.

title: VLM server entry points are thin wrappers that delegate to vllm/lmdeploy OpenAI-compatible servers
description: The `mineru-vllm-server`, `mineru-lmdeploy-server`, and `mineru-openai-server` entry points in pyproject.toml are thin wrappers. `openai_server` detects available inference framework (vllm/lmdeploy) with `auto` mode and delegates to the framework's own OpenAI-compatible server. MinerU does not implement its own LLM serving — it re-exports the upstream inference framework's server, just selecting which one to start. The VLM backend's `http-client` mode then connects to any OpenAI-compatible URL including these servers or third-party endpoints.
relevance_notes: The 'wrap and delegate to OpenAI-compatible server' pattern means the inference backend is swappable without changing client code. The auto-detection (try vllm, fall back to lmdeploy) is directly applicable to any optional-dependency inference service.

title: MCP server is a separate pip package (mineru-mcp), invoked via uvx, not in this repo
description: The MCP server is referenced in docs as `uvx mineru-mcp` — it is a separate package, not part of the `mineru` source tree. No MCP server code exists in `mineru/cli/`. The entry point `mineru-mcp` is absent from `pyproject.toml`. MCP integration is a separate distribution that presumably wraps the mineru FastAPI client or CLI. This is consistent with the README listing 'MCP Server' as a feature but the source containing no MCP implementation.
relevance_notes: Relevant for understanding the integration boundary: the MCP layer is a thin client wrapper over the HTTP API, not a tight internal integration. This is the right architectural choice — MCP tools invoke the existing REST API rather than embedding inference code.

title: Batch OCR detection groups crops by resolution stride (64px) for uniform padding in batches
description: In the hybrid backend's batch OCR path, cropped images are grouped by `(target_h, target_w)` where both dimensions are rounded up to the nearest 64-pixel stride. Within each group, images are zero-padded to the group's target size, forming a uniform batch tensor. This avoids the overhead of per-image resize while minimizing padding waste. `RESOLUTION_GROUP_STRIDE = 64` is a module-level constant. The batch size within each group is further capped by `batch_ratio * OCR_DET_BASE_BATCH_SIZE`.
relevance_notes: Directly portable to any batch inference pipeline processing variable-size crops. The stride-aligned grouping pattern is more efficient than per-image dynamic batching or sorting by exact size. Replaces the need for a full dynamic batching framework for this use case.

title: Async pipeline backend uses asyncio.to_thread for CPU-bound parsing to avoid event loop blocking
description: In `run_parse_job`, the `pipeline` backend uses `await asyncio.to_thread(do_parse, **parse_kwargs)` while non-pipeline backends use `await aio_do_parse(...)` (which has native async page loop). This correctly offloads the CPU-bound pipeline work to a thread pool without blocking the FastAPI event loop. File I/O for uploads is also offloaded via `asyncio.to_thread(load_parse_inputs, uploads)`. The pattern is consistent: synchronous CPU work goes through `asyncio.to_thread`, async inference engines get native coroutines.
relevance_notes: Clear template: synchronous legacy backends get `asyncio.to_thread` wrapping; new async backends implement native coroutines. The delineation at the `run_parse_job` boundary is clean and avoids infecting the async surface with blocking calls.

one_liner: MinerU v3.0.0 demonstrates production-grade patterns for multi-backend AI inference: configurable sliding-window memory management, load-score routing with jitter and subprocess GPU isolation, keyed singleton model cache with multi-path shutdown, IO abstraction for S3/file interchangeability, and clean asyncio.to_thread/native-coroutine delineation — 11 of 14 findings are E0-E1 extractability.

title: HTTP API has no authentication or authorization
observation: The mineru-api FastAPI service exposes POST /file_parse, POST /tasks, GET /tasks/{id}, GET /tasks/{id}/result, and GET /health with no auth middleware, no API key check, no Bearer/JWT validation. Any caller that reaches the port can submit parse jobs and retrieve results. Default bind is 127.0.0.1 but operators can pass --host 0.0.0.0.
implication: If deployed on a reachable interface (a common production scenario for a multi-GPU parse service) it is an unauthenticated file-upload and compute endpoint. Task IDs are UUIDv4 but the service trusts anyone who can call it — there is no tenant model at all.

title: No request-level rate limiting on parse endpoints
observation: Concurrency is capped by an asyncio.Semaphore (MINERU_API_MAX_CONCURRENT_REQUESTS, default 3) but there is no per-IP or per-caller rate limit. A single client can enqueue arbitrarily many pending tasks on POST /tasks; tasks accumulate in memory until task_retention_seconds (default 24h) elapses.
implication: Trivial DoS/compute-exhaustion vector: flood /tasks with large PDFs and fill GPU queues, disk (uploads written to MINERU_API_OUTPUT_ROOT), and the in-memory task dict. No backpressure beyond concurrent execution.

title: No upload size limit on multipart file endpoints
observation: save_upload_files() streams UploadFile chunks (1MB each) to disk with no enforced maximum file size, no total-request-size limit, and no count limit on the files[] array. FastAPI/Starlette does not impose a default. After saving, guess_suffix_by_path enforces allowed types but only AFTER the bytes are on disk.
implication: A single request can fill the disk (denial of service) and waste I/O. Combined with the absence of auth this is unauthenticated disk exhaustion. Operators must configure a reverse proxy to enforce limits — not enforced in the app itself.

title: No CORS or TrustedHost middleware configured
observation: Only GZipMiddleware is installed. There is no CORSMiddleware and no TrustedHostMiddleware on either mineru-api or mineru-router. FastAPI default is same-origin-only for browser JSON, but /tasks accepts multipart form data which CORS does not preflight for simple requests with the default content type.
implication: If an operator exposes mineru-api alongside a browser UI on another origin they will likely add permissive CORS themselves. The library ships without a safe default and without Host header validation, which enables DNS rebinding against a 127.0.0.1 instance.

title: OpenAPI/docs endpoints enabled by default
observation: create_app() enables /openapi.json, /docs, and /redoc by default (MINERU_API_ENABLE_FASTAPI_DOCS defaults to True). Same in router.py create_app at line 1290.
implication: On an exposed deployment this gives attackers a free schema of every endpoint and parameter. Not exploitable by itself but is information disclosure and makes attack tooling trivial. Should default to off for production.

title: server_url form field is a user-controlled outbound URL (SSRF)
observation: The server_url form field (used by vlm-http-client / hybrid-http-client backends) is a client-supplied string passed through to the VLM backend as an OpenAI-compatible base URL. There is no allowlist, no scheme restriction, and no block for internal IPs (127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254 metadata endpoint, RFC1918).
implication: Classic SSRF: an authenticated (or, given finding #1, unauthenticated) caller can cause the MinerU process to issue HTTP requests to arbitrary internal hosts — cloud metadata services, internal admin panels, etc. Response bodies may leak through parse errors.

title: Router upstream URLs are used verbatim with follow_redirects=True
observation: submit_payload_to_upstream_sync and proxy_router_task_result use httpx with follow_redirects=True against upstream base URLs that came from env MINERU_ROUTER_UPSTREAM_URLS_JSON or --upstream-url. A compromised or misconfigured upstream can 3xx-redirect the router to an internal target; the router will follow.
implication: Low-probability but viable lateral movement if any upstream is attacker-influenced. Configuration-only mitigation; no code-level guardrail.

title: HTTP data reader does not verify TLS status or size
observation: HttpReader.read uses requests.get(url).content with no timeout, no size cap, and no streaming — entire response is buffered into memory. HttpWriter asserts 200-299 but does not set a timeout either. These helpers are used as pluggable data readers for parse inputs.
implication: A URL-driven parse pipeline can be hung indefinitely by a slow upstream (DoS) and can OOM on huge responses. Standard requests library footgun, but notable for a document-parsing service that frequently fetches by URL.

title: ZIP extraction is correctly path-safe
observation: safe_extract_zip in api_client.py explicitly rejects absolute paths and '..' components and re-validates resolved paths against the output root. This is the correct posture against zip-slip and mirrors the CLI download path.
implication: Positive finding: one of the few places in the codebase that gets path safety right. Worth calling out because the same care is not visible elsewhere.

title: FileBasedDataWriter accepts absolute paths bypassing parent_dir sandbox
observation: FileBasedDataWriter.write checks 'if not os.path.isabs(fn_path)' before joining parent_dir — absolute paths are passed through unchanged. Relative paths are joined but not validated, so '..' components still escape. No symlink check.
implication: Library-only concern: a caller that passes untrusted file paths gets arbitrary filesystem write. The FastAPI layer does not expose this directly, but any embedding app that wires user input into a FileBasedDataWriter has an unbounded write primitive. Documented behavior, but dangerous default.

title: S3 credentials handled only via constructor args, no env fallback
observation: S3Reader/S3Writer take ak/sk as positional constructor arguments and pass them directly to boto3.client. There is no env-var fallback, no credential provider chain, and no logging of credentials. MultiBucketS3DataReader holds them on self and in a per-bucket client cache.
implication: Positive-ish: no hardcoded creds, no leakage to logs observed. Negative: forces consumers to pass secrets around in code rather than using boto3 default credential chain (IAM role, ~/.aws/credentials). Encourages embedding secrets in config files.

title: config_version string comparison is lexicographic
observation: download_and_modify_json compares config_version with 'if config_version < "1.3.1"' — this is a string compare, not semver. '1.10.0' < '1.3.1' is True, so a newer config would be clobbered by a re-download.
implication: Reliability bug more than security, but it means an operator's locally modified config can be silently overwritten by a re-fetch from gcore.jsdelivr.net. Trust-in-CDN implicit.

title: Model config fetched from public CDN without integrity check
observation: configure_model() downloads mineru.template.json from https://gcore.jsdelivr.net/gh/opendatalab/MinerU@master/mineru.template.json with no checksum, no signature, no pinned commit. download_json uses requests.get with no timeout. The fetched JSON is then merged into ~/mineru.json and used to configure model paths.
implication: Supply-chain risk: if the jsdelivr mirror is compromised or the master branch is tampered with, the config is trusted blindly. Integrity is delegated entirely to TLS + the attacker not controlling the repo. Pin by commit SHA or verify a signature.

title: Model downloads delegated to huggingface_hub and modelscope
observation: Actual model weight downloads route through huggingface_hub and modelscope libraries via auto_download_and_get_model_root_path. Those libraries enforce HTTPS and (for HF) optional ETag verification. MinerU itself does not implement download logic, so trust is inherited from those packages.
implication: Appropriate delegation — these libraries are the de facto standard. Operators who care about provenance should enable HF_HUB_ENABLE_HF_TRANSFER=0 and pinned revisions, which MinerU does not configure.

title: SECURITY.md is minimal but functional
observation: SECURITY.md points to GitHub's private vulnerability reporting (https://github.com/opendatalab/MinerU/security), requests standard repro info, lists English and Chinese as preferred languages, and commits to pre-disclosure confidentiality. No explicit SLA, no scope statement, no PGP key.
implication: Meets the bar for a well-maintained open-source project but does not document scope (what's in/out of bounds — e.g., is the Gradio app in scope? the router? model weights?). No bounty. Acceptable for AGPL OSS.

title: Dependency list uses sensible upper bounds but several unpinned
observation: 38 core deps; most use '>=X,<Y' caret-style bounds (good). However 'requests', 'httpx', 'uvicorn', 'reportlab', 'python-multipart', and 'fastapi' are completely unpinned (no lower or upper bound). Optional 'gradio' dep enumerates ~10 specific excluded versions — suggests unstable upstream.
implication: Unpinned fastapi and python-multipart in particular are high-blast-radius (auth/upload handling). A breaking or regressed release will ship to downstream users immediately. Recommend >=min,<next-major on all direct deps.

title: subprocess calls are safe (argv lists, no shell=True)
observation: All subprocess.Popen and subprocess.run calls in api_client.py and router.py use list-form argv with sys.executable as the binary. No shell=True anywhere in mineru/cli. taskkill on Windows uses list form with numeric PID. No user input is interpolated into command lines.
implication: No command injection surface in the CLI/router subprocess management. Positive finding.

title: Uploads normalized to basename before disk write
observation: normalize_upload_filename uses Path(upload_name).name to strip directory components from client-supplied filenames, then applies a UTF-8 byte-length truncation. build_upload_destination re-uses this and handles collisions by appending __upload_N suffixes.
implication: Upload path traversal via filename is blocked. Positive finding — the library understands the risk and addresses it consistently in fast_api.py and router.py.

title: GitHub release workflow uses hardcoded action SHA, but cli.yml pins to tags
observation: python-package.yml pins softprops/action-gh-release to a full commit SHA (good). Other actions (actions/checkout@v6, setup-python@v6, upload-artifact@v6, setup-uv@v7) are pinned to major version tags, which can silently move. PYPI_TOKEN and RELEASE_TOKEN are referenced via secrets (correct).
implication: Standard OSS workflow posture. Supply-chain-wise, tag-pinned third-party actions are a known risk but industry-acceptable. Consider dependabot for actions.

title: Gradio app launched without auth or allowed_paths restriction
observation: demo.launch() is called with only server_name, server_port, i18n, and show_api kwargs. No auth= tuple, no allowed_paths=, no ssl_keyfile. Defaults to 127.0.0.1 when server_name is None but operators can set any host.
implication: Same pattern as fast_api: safe-ish default, dangerous when exposed. Gradio's default allowed_paths is restrictive, but the lack of auth and the ease of --server-name 0.0.0.0 means misconfiguration is one flag away from exposing an unauth UI that uploads files to compute.

one_liner: MinerU v3.0.0's HTTP surface is a functional document-parsing service with solid upload-path hardening and safe subprocess usage, but it ships with zero authentication, no rate limiting, no upload size caps, no CORS/TrustedHost middleware, docs enabled by default, and a user-controlled server_url field that is a textbook SSRF primitive — acceptable as a library/local tool, risky the moment it is exposed beyond localhost.

title: mkdocs Structure: Sensible but Redundant Nav Definition
observation: The nav section is defined twice — once nested under 'Home' and again as flat top-level tabs. This is an mkdocs-material pattern for enabling both a home landing index and tab navigation, but it creates visual duplication in the source file. Sections are logically ordered: Quick Start -> Usage -> Reference -> FAQ -> Demo. Bilingual i18n is implemented via the mkdocs-i18n plugin with a folder-based docs_structure. Nav translation keys for zh are fully declared.

title: Bilingual Coverage: English First-Class, Chinese Has Hardware Extras
observation: The mkdocs i18n plugin is configured with fallback_to_default=true, meaning untranslated zh pages fall back to en. The zh tree contains at least one directory not present in en: docs/zh/usage/acceleration_cards/ with per-chip guides (Ascend.md confirmed). The en tree has no equivalent hardware-acceleration sub-pages. Chinese users get more hardware detail; English users are pointed to the same pages without a clear signal that zh has additional content.

title: Getting Started: 3 Commands to First Parse — Acceptable
observation: From install to first parse: (1) pip install uv, (2) uv pip install 'mineru[all]', (3) mineru -p <input> -o <output>. The README hardware-requirements table precedes the install commands, which helps users pick the right backend before installing. The fallback CPU command (-b pipeline) is shown immediately after the default command. Time-to-first-success is roughly 5-10 minutes depending on model download time, which is not documented. No model download step is shown in Quick Start — it happens implicitly on first run.

title: Backend Selection: No Decision Matrix, Requires Cross-Reading
observation: Five backends exist (pipeline, hybrid-auto-engine, hybrid-http-client, vlm-auto-engine, vlm-http-client). The quick_start hardware table compares them on accuracy (86+ vs 90+), CPU support, VRAM, RAM, and disk. However there is no explicit decision matrix or 'which backend should I choose' section. A user must infer that hybrid-auto-engine is the default, that pipeline is the CPU fallback, and that http-client variants require a separately running server. The accuracy metric source (OmniDocBench v1.5) is footnoted but not linked to a live benchmark result.

title: Docker Deployment: Solid Coverage of Four Compose Profiles
observation: Four Docker Compose profiles are documented (openai-server, api, gradio, router) with concrete one-liner launch commands and TIP blocks showing how to verify each service. The GPU prerequisite (Volta+, CUDA 12.9.1+, 8GB VRAM) is called out before the run command. A docker run example for interactive use is also provided. Multi-GPU router setup (--local-gpus auto vs --upstream-url) is covered in both the Docker doc and quick_usage.md without duplication of the core explanation.

title: Hardware Acceleration: 11 Chips Claimed, Only zh Has Per-Chip Guides
observation: The changelog lists 11 domestic Chinese AI chips (Ascend, T-Head, METAX, Hygon, Enflame, Moore Threads, IluvatarCorex, Cambricon, Kunlunxin, Tecorigin, Biren) as supported as of 2.7.6. The Ascend.md guide in zh is detailed: tested platform info, two Dockerfile paths (vllm vs lmdeploy), full docker run command with device mounts, and a compatibility matrix by backend and container type. English docs have zero per-chip pages. An English user has no documented path for any of these chips unless they read Chinese.

title: API Reference: REST Endpoints Documented Inline in quick_usage.md
observation: POST /tasks, POST /file_parse, GET /tasks/{task_id}, GET /tasks/{task_id}/result, and GET /health are all documented in a single TIP block with curl examples for both async and sync patterns. Behavioral semantics are explained: task retention (24h default), no cross-restart persistence, queued_ahead field, and environment variables for tuning retention and cleanup intervals. The doc also points to http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs for live OpenAPI UI. This is thorough for inline documentation, but it is buried inside a TIP admonition rather than a dedicated API reference page.

title: API Reference: No Dedicated Page, Schema Not Formally Specified
observation: The Reference section contains only two pages: output_files.md and changelog.md. There is no standalone API reference page. Request/response schemas for POST /tasks and POST /file_parse are not documented as formal schemas — users must infer field names from curl examples (files=@, return_md=true, response_format_zip=true, return_original_file=true). The OpenAPI UI at /docs is deferred to as the live reference, which is reasonable but means the docs are not self-contained offline.

title: Output File Reference: Exceptionally Detailed with Real JSON Examples
observation: output_files.md is the strongest page in the docs. It documents model.json, middle.json, content_list.json, content_list_v2.json, layout.pdf, and span.pdf for both pipeline and vlm backends separately. Each file includes: naming format, field tables with types and descriptions, block hierarchy diagrams, coordinate system notes, and multi-entry JSON samples with realistic values. A backward-compatibility warning for VLM 2.5 output changes is present. The page also includes a consumer-oriented summary table mapping use cases (debugging, content extraction, secondary development) to specific files.

title: Changelog: User-Facing and Feature-Rich, Not Git-Log Dumps
observation: Changelog entries describe capabilities in user terms: 'OCR speed significantly improved 200-300%', 'Added hybrid backend combining pipeline and vlm strengths', 'Switched default backend from pipeline to hybrid-auto-engine'. Bug fixes include GitHub issue numbers (#4300, #4168) for traceability. CVE references are included for security fixes. Entries are grouped by series (2.7, 2.6, 2.5) with sub-version granularity. The tone is specific and informative rather than terse. Breaking changes are called out inline (e.g., VLM 2.5 output not backward-compatible).

title: Demo Page: Iframe Only, No Walkthrough or Example PDFs
observation: The Demo page is a single iframe embedding the HuggingFace Spaces demo. There are no example PDFs linked, no annotated screenshots, no Colab notebook walk-through embedded in the docs (though one exists on GitHub as a gist linked from the README badges). A user who wants to understand what good vs bad output looks like before installing must navigate away from the docs entirely.

title: FAQ: Only Two Entries, Defers to External AI Tool
observation: The FAQ contains exactly two entries: a WSL2 libGL error and a CJK font loss issue on Linux. The page header directs users to DeepWiki's AI assistant for anything not listed. For a project with 58k stars and thousands of GitHub issues, two FAQ entries is a significant gap. Common expected topics absent: model download failures, CUDA version mismatches, Windows-specific issues, performance tuning, DOCX limitations, API rate/concurrency questions.

title: Contribution Docs: Absent from the Docs Site
observation: The README contains an 'All Thanks To Our Contributors' section with a contrib.rocks image but no link to CONTRIBUTING.md, no issue templates described, and no code of conduct referenced. The docs site has no Contributing section in the nav. SECURITY.md exists and is well-structured (responsible disclosure, required report fields, preferred languages). Contributor guidance beyond 'submit an issue with the relevant document attached' is missing.

title: Environment Variables: Comprehensive but Split Across Two Pages
observation: cli_tools.md documents 18 environment variables with defaults and behavioral notes. quick_usage.md documents additional API-tier variables (MINERU_API_TASK_RETENTION_SECONDS etc.) inline. There is no single reference table of all environment variables. A developer configuring production deployment must read both pages and cross-reference. The variables themselves are well-named and well-documented individually.

title: Known Issues: Honest and Prominent in README
observation: The README contains a 'Known Issues' section listing 7 concrete limitations: reading order failures in complex layouts, vertical text limits, uncommon list formats, code block layout model gaps, unsupported document types (comics, art albums, primary school textbooks), table row/column errors in complex tables, and OCR accuracy for lesser-known languages. This level of candor about limitations is a documentation strength that sets accurate user expectations.

one_liner: MinerU docs are substantially above average for an open-source ML tool: the output format reference and API usage docs are genuinely excellent, getting-started is fast, and the changelog is user-facing rather than a git dump — but the FAQ is nearly empty, hardware acceleration content exists only in Chinese, the demo page is an iframe with no walkthroughs, backend selection requires inference across multiple pages, and there is no formal REST schema or contribution guide.

title: Entire test suite is a single end-to-end function
observation: The `tests/` tree contains exactly one pytest file (`tests/unittest/test_e2e.py`) with one test function `test_pipeline_with_two_config`. There are no unit tests, no integration tests, no separate e2e suite, no `__init__.py`, and no `conftest.py`. For a 58k-star document parsing engine with pipeline/vlm/vllm/lmdeploy/mlx/gradio backends, the breadth-to-depth ratio of testing is extremely low.

title: Coverage threshold set to 0.2%
observation: `get_coverage.py` parses `htmlcov/index.html` and asserts `percentage_float >= 0.2` — literally 0.2 percent. This is the only coverage gate in CI. Any regression that doesn't break the single e2e test passes unnoticed regardless of coverage delta. There is no Codecov upload, no per-module coverage, and no PR coverage comment.

title: Primary test workflow only runs on tags, not PRs
observation: `python-package.yml` triggers only on `push: tags: ['*released']` and `workflow_dispatch`. It runs `pip install -e .[core]` across Python 3.10-3.13 but never executes pytest — it only verifies installation succeeds. No pull request or push-to-main trigger exists for this workflow, so the Python matrix never validates actual test behavior.

title: Actual test workflow runs only on dev branch push, single Python version
observation: `cli.yml` is the only workflow that runs `coverage run` (executing the single e2e test). Triggers are `push` to `master` or `dev` only — no `pull_request` trigger. Pull requests therefore never run the test suite before merge. It uses a single Python 3.12 + ubuntu-latest with no matrix, despite the project supporting 3.10-3.13 and claiming Windows/macOS/Linux compatibility (mlx/vllm/lmdeploy per-OS extras).

title: coverage command_line hardcodes e2e as sole target
observation: `[tool.coverage.run] command_line = "-m pytest tests/unittest/test_e2e.py"` hardcodes the single e2e file as the coverage entrypoint. This quirk is the mechanism by which coverage is measured: `coverage run` (with no args) reads command_line and invokes that exact pytest command. It implies e2e is the canonical and only coverage target — anything not exercised by the PDF-parse-and-compare flow is invisible to coverage.

title: Coverage omits user-facing CLI and server surfaces
observation: `[tool.coverage.run] omit` excludes `gradio_app.py`, `models_download.py`, `fast_api.py`, `cli/client.py`, `vlm_vllm_server.py`, `cli_parser.py`. These are the primary user entrypoints (Gradio UI, FastAPI server, CLI client, model downloader). Excluding them from coverage means the surfaces users actually touch are formally untested and not even counted against the 0.2% floor.

title: Fuzzy-match assertions as regression oracle
observation: Regression detection relies on `fuzzywuzzy.fuzz.ratio(...) > 90` on extracted text plus substring checks (`"$$"`, `"lambda"`, `"frac"`) and a `len(type_set) >= 4` coverage-of-element-types check. There is no golden-file snapshot comparison, no JSON schema validation, and no per-element diff. The approach tolerates silent quality regressions as long as 90% fuzzy similarity holds for a handful of hardcoded strings from one PDF.

title: Single test PDF as the entire fixture corpus
observation: The test fixture corpus is one file: `tests/unittest/pdfs/test.pdf`. There are no DOCX, PPTX, image, multi-column, scanned, CJK-language, or malformed-input fixtures — despite the project supporting all of these formats and explicitly advertising DOCX/PPTX parsing via `python-docx`, `pypptx-with-oxml`, `mammoth`. Backend divergence (pipeline txt vs ocr vs vlm) is partially covered but only against this one English PDF about Trump and Wharton.

title: No conftest.py, no fixtures, no parametrization
observation: There is no `conftest.py` at any level, no `fixtures/` directory, no `@pytest.fixture` usage, and no `@pytest.mark.parametrize`. The two pipeline modes (`txt`, `ocr`) are tested via sequential hardcoded function calls inside one test. Adding a new backend or document type requires editing the monolithic test rather than adding a fixture/parameter.

title: No mocking of model loading, no slow/gpu markers
observation: The test invokes `pipeline_doc_analyze_streaming` which loads real ML models (ONNX/layout/OCR) via `modelscope`/`huggingface-hub`. There is no mocking, no `@pytest.mark.slow`, no `@pytest.mark.gpu`, and no `skipif` for CI-without-GPU. The 240-minute CI timeout (`timeout-minutes: 240`) confirms the suite is expected to take hours. Developers without models cannot run tests locally in a reasonable timeframe.

title: pytest -s disables output capture globally
observation: `[tool.pytest.ini_options] addopts = "-s --cov=mineru --cov-report html"` hardcodes `-s` (no capture). This forces every test run to stream stdout/stderr, making CI logs noisy and preventing pytest from capturing per-test output for failure diagnosis. It also hardcodes `--cov-report html` only — no terminal report, no XML for Codecov, no lcov.

title: Auto-rerun on failure masks flakiness
observation: `rerun.yml` automatically re-runs failed jobs up to 3 attempts (`run_attempt < 3`) after a 600s sleep when the `ci` workflow fails on master. This silently converts flaky test failures into passes without surfacing the flakiness rate. No flake-tracking, no issue creation, no dashboard — failed-then-passed runs look identical to clean runs in the UI.

title: Test infra helpers are brittle shell glue
observation: `get_coverage.py` scrapes the coverage HTML report by parsing `htmlcov/index.html` with BeautifulSoup to find a `<span class='pc_cov'>`. This depends on an internal coverage.py HTML template that can change between versions. A simpler `coverage report --fail-under=0.2` would be one line and immune to template churn. `clean_coverage.py` has a logic bug: `if not os.path.exists(path): if os.path.isfile(path):` can never be true.

title: Install-matrix job does validate cross-version compatibility
observation: Despite the actual test job being single-version, the `check-install` job does run `pip install -e .[core]` across Python 3.10/3.11/3.12/3.13 with `fail-fast: false`. This at least catches dependency-resolution and syntax errors across supported Python versions, even if no tests execute. It's the only multi-version signal in CI.

title: test dependencies scoped via optional-dependencies
observation: `[project.optional-dependencies].test = ["mineru[core]", "pytest", "pytest-cov", "coverage", "fuzzywuzzy"]` keeps test tooling out of the default install and composes cleanly with the `core` extra. This is correct modern packaging even if the test content itself is minimal.

one_liner: One e2e test, one PDF fixture, 0.2% coverage floor, PRs don't run tests, and a flake-retry workflow silently masks failures — the test infrastructure is essentially a smoke test dressed as a coverage system.

title: 11 Dockerfiles following identical template (1 global + 10 China vendor variants)
observation: Each variant bases off a vendor-specific pre-built image (Ascend/DCU/Kunlunxin/METAX/Moore Threads/Cambricon/Enflame/Iluvatar/T-Head PPU) containing vLLM or LMDeploy. Recipe: vendor base -> Noto CJK fonts -> pip install mineru[core]>=3.0.0 with numpy==1.26.4 + opencv-python==4.11.0.86 pinned -> mineru-models-download -s modelscope. Some variants carry sed patches inline (kxpu rewrites nn.GELU in qwen2_vl.py, maca has torchvision rename hack, mlu toggles vllm/lmdeploy via ARG BACKEND).
implication: Maintenance cost: 11 Dockerfiles to keep current. Distribution model: vendor maintains base image, MinerU layers on top. Pattern captured as kno-mineru-007 (T3).

title: docker/compose.yaml binds all services to 0.0.0.0 by default
observation: 4 service profiles (openai-server port 30000, api port 8000, router port 8002, gradio port 7860) all configured with --host 0.0.0.0 --server-name 0.0.0.0. No auth, no TLS, no reverse proxy. Healthcheck via curl -f http://localhost:PORT/health.
implication: Reinforces anti-mineru-002 (unarmored HTTP defaults). A user running docker compose --profile router up exposes unauthenticated multi-GPU parse endpoint on their network by default.

title: 13 per-chip deployment guides follow identical structural template
observation: Structure: section 1 test platform -> section 2 Dockerfile build recipe -> section 3 docker run with vendor device mounts + env vars -> section 4 support matrix per backend x engine combo -> Tips (vendor VISIBLE_DEVICES env + smi command). Each vendor has its own _VISIBLE_DEVICES: ASCEND_RT, SUPA, MLU, TOPS, XPU, SDAA, MTHREADS.
implication: Template is reusable. Structural discipline is high. But all 13 are Chinese-only - see anti-mineru-004 documentation parity gap.

title: AMD.md is community Triton-kernel workaround doc (the only guide with code)
observation: 360-line community contribution explaining that MIOpen on AMD RDNA is missing an optimized Conv3d(bfloat16) kernel for Qwen2VisionPatchEmbed. Provides TWO full Triton kernel implementations inline: 3D patchify kernel and matrix-multiplication variant tuned for 7900xtx (BLOCK_M=128, BLOCK_N=128, BLOCK_K=32, num_stages=4, num_warps=8). Also patches mineru_vl_utils/vlm_client/vllm_async_engine_client.py for missing get_lora_tokenizer() method.
implication: Only MinerU doc that ships actual code. Shows MinerU team accepts community work that lives inside upstream vllm source tree, not their own codebase. File as reference for pragmatic ML-debugging documentation.

title: VastAI.md structurally different and version-pinned to MinerU 2.7.0
observation: Different section numbering (1-5, company/platform/env/features/notes) than the other 12 guides (1-4, platform/env/container/notes). Pinned to MinerU 2.7.0 specifically, not 3.0. Only vlm-auto-engine and vlm-http-client are supported - every pipeline and hybrid scenario is blocked.
implication: The 10+ chip support README claim has caveats. VastAI is counted but cannot run the default backend. File in anti-mineru-007 (README claims vs reality).

title: Mixed inference runtime per model type (ONNX/Torch/vLLM)
observation: Lightweight classifiers use ONNX (paddle_ori_cls, paddle_table_cls - orientation 0/90/180/270, wired vs wireless tables, identical 256->224 preprocessing). Heavy models use PyTorch + HuggingFace transformers (pp_doclayoutv2 built on RTDetrForObjectDetection + LayoutLMv3TextEmbeddings, 25 labeled block types with per-class confidence thresholds). VLM serving uses vLLM via thin CLI wrapper. Central download via auto_download_and_get_model_root_path with ModelPath enum.
implication: Pattern captured as pat-mineru-005 (T3). Each model size matched to appropriate inference runtime. Reference pattern for ML serving tools.

title: vLLM CLI wrapping injects defaults without forking
observation: 70-line wrapper around vllm.entrypoints.cli.main. Intercepts --model/--port/--gpu-memory-utilization/--logits-processors, applies per-device arg mutation via mod_kwargs_by_device_type, auto-injects mineru_vl_utils:MinerULogitsProcessor, sets OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 if unset, hands off to vllm_main().
implication: Cheap maintainable alternative to forking vLLM. Pattern captured as pat-mineru-006 (T2). Directly applicable to SoNash for wrapping third-party CLIs.

title: 109-language OCR claim decomposes to enumerated language families
observation: latin_lang = 48 languages, arabic_lang = 8 languages, cyrillic_lang = 16+ languages, plus Chinese/Japanese/Korean/Thai/Tamil/Telugu/Kannada/Georgian families. Each list is explicit, not dynamically generated.
implication: The 109-language README claim is backed by enumerated language lists. Contrast with the MCP Server claim (anti-mineru-007) which is not backed by first-party code.

title: demo.py shows full async client contract
observation: 256-line example: LocalAPIServer auto-start when api_url is None, submit_parse_task with form data, wait_for_task_result with on_status_update callback, queued_ahead counter in response, four state transitions (pending/processing/completed/failed) surfaced via TaskStatusSnapshot, download_result_zip, safe_extract_zip helper (zip-slip defense). WSL drvfs workaround: redirects TMPDIR to /tmp when current tempdir is under /mnt/ to avoid vLLM/ZeroMQ IPC socket failures.
implication: Consumer-side reference for pat-mineru-001. The client state-UX is as important as the server state machine.

title: Unpinned GitHub Action in mkdocs deploy workflow
observation: Uses mhausenblas/mkdocs-deploy-gh-pages@master (unpinned) for docs publishing. cla.yml pins contributor-assistant/github-action@v2.6.1 - inconsistent pinning discipline. Uses secrets.RELEASE_TOKEN.
implication: Supply-chain gap. Compromise of upstream action would let attackers publish arbitrary content to docs site with RELEASE_TOKEN permissions. Promoted to anti-mineru-008.

title: cla.yml uses pull_request_target with bounded allowlist
observation: Uses pull_request_target trigger (security-sensitive, can access secrets on fork PRs) but bounded by 14-person allowlist and scoped to CLA signing. Uses contributor-assistant/github-action@v2.6.1 (pinned version). contents: write, pull-requests: write permissions.
implication: Normal CLA bot pattern. Exposure is bounded by the allowlist. Not a finding requiring action.

title: Pillow floor-only version constraint with known CVE in exposed range
observation: pillow>=11.0.0 with no upper bound. CVE-2026-25990 (PSD out-of-bounds write in Photoshop Document handler, affects 10.3.0 to before 12.1.1, fixed in 12.1.1). MinerU floor (11.0.0) is below the fix version, so the installed Pillow depends on resolver choice at install time. PSD handler is compiled into Pillow by default.
implication: Not exploitable in the common PDF/DOCX parse path, but reachable if MinerU is ever fed PSD files. Fix: ranged constraint pillow>=12.1.1,<13. Promoted to anti-mineru-009.

title: pdfminer.six CVE-2025-64512 (pickle RCE) is patched via floor constraint
observation: pdfminer.six>=20251230 is the floor, which matches the CVE-2025-64512 fix release. The changelog entry for v2.7.1 explicitly mentions the update. The underlying library switched CMap storage from pickle to json.
implication: Good discipline: CVE noted + fix applied + floor enforced. Contrast with the unbounded Pillow constraint.

title: lxml XXE risk is usage-dependent, not audited
observation: lxml>=4.0.0,<7.0.0 has generic XXE risk because default XMLParser allows entity resolution and network loading. MinerU imports lxml.etree in docx_converter.py for parsing DOCX XML. Whether the actual usage enables unsafe features (resolve_entities=True, no_network=False) was not audited at source-line level during this Standard-depth pass.
implication: Latent risk - needs source audit. Not blocking, but worth filing for a future deep-dive if MinerU is ever fed attacker-controlled DOCX files.

title: MinerU 2.5 arxiv paper: coarse-to-fine two-stage parsing
observation: 61-author paper on a 1.2B-parameter VLM (smaller than most document-parsing VLMs). Two-stage strategy decouples global layout analysis (stage 1, downsampled images, cheap structural pass) from local content recognition (stage 2, native-resolution crops, preserves detail where it matters). Claims state-of-the-art accuracy at lower compute than competing VLMs.
implication: Framing analogue for SoNash depth tiers (Quick -> Standard -> Deep). Coarse-to-fine discipline generalizes. Captured as kno-mineru-009 (T2).

title: MinerU 1.x arxiv paper: PDF-Extract-Kit + pre/post-processing
observation: 18-author paper describing the pipeline backend theoretical grounding. Integrates PDF-Extract-Kit models as foundation plus domain-specific pre/post-processing rules. Modular architecture (OCR, layout, formula recognition as independent components) rather than end-to-end.
implication: Background reading only. Low direct relevance to SoNash (we do not train extraction models).

title: FIRST-PASS ERROR: opendatalab/mineru-mcp does not exist
file: creator-view.md original Section 2 + value-map.json (original kno candidates)
observation: First-pass Creator View referenced mineru-mcp as a viable T2 knowledge candidate. Verification: GitHub API returns 404 for opendatalab/mineru-mcp. Changelog does not mention MCP anywhere. The MCP Server claim is only in the README integration matrix. Top community implementation is neosun100/mineru-mcp-server (9 stars, no license, single maintainer, Python/FastMCP). None are owned by opendatalab.
implication: Corrected in creator-view.md Appendix A12. Demoted from T2 knowledge candidate to anti-mineru-007 (README integration claim unbacked by first-party code). This is a lesson in verifying README claims before citing them.

title: crawl4ai is the actual progressive-extraction reference, not MinerU
observation: crawl4ai (Apache-2.0, 63k stars, Python, quality 83) explicitly implements resume_state + on_state_change callbacks for crash recovery on long-running crawls. MinerU POST /tasks uses in-memory dict that does NOT survive restart. License matrix: MinerU/firecrawl AGPL-3.0, marker/surya GPL-3.0, crawl4ai Apache-2.0. crawl4ai is the only one SoNash can adopt as a direct dependency.
implication: Rewrote pat-mineru-001 recommendation. MinerU state machine is a shape reference only; the adoptable implementation lives in crawl4ai. See creator-view.md Appendix A15 for full matrix.

title: MinerU stands out at the bottom of the cluster on test discipline
observation: MinerU tests: F/18 (1 file, 1 function, 0.2% coverage floor). firecrawl: A/85 (capability-gated tests, harness-driven). crawl4ai: A (inferred from maintainability/process). marker: B (inferred). surya: C (inferred). MinerU is uniquely bad within the document-parsing cluster, not just typical for open-source ML.
implication: The test story in MinerU cannot be excused as common in the space. Peer projects in the exact same domain have real test infrastructure.
